summary: I grew up in Hong Kong, where yum cha was a big part of my childhood and cultural identity. I remember eating har gow and cheung fun there, and the feeling of contentment from being with my family during those long meals. Hong Kong shaped my early experiences and my sense of self before my mom and I moved to Atlanta in 2006.

In 2006, my mom and I resettled in Atlanta, in a predominantly white neighborhood where Southern and American foods were everywhere. It was quite a change from Hong Kong, and the closest Chinese community was a 45-minute drive away, so yum cha disappeared from my life. Despite this, I adapted and started embracing new foods and experiences in Atlanta.

I shared my immigrant experiences with my best friend while eating tteokbokki back in eighth grade. That moment was important because it combined my personal story with a food that wasn’t from my own culture, showing how my identity was expanding. Tteokbokki became a symbol of connection and sharing during my early teenage years.

During high school, my mom and I dreamed about future travels while eating bowls of Tonkotsu ramen. It was a comforting ritual that connected us through food and hope for what was to come. Tonkotsu ramen represented both a culinary and emotional bridge during those formative years.

Chicken and waffles was the dish my classmates and I shared while commiserating about college applications. This Southern comfort food became part of my social life and a backdrop to important conversations during high school. It showed how my palate and friendships were evolving in Atlanta.

In college, my friends and I bonded over slices of tres leches cake while exchanging advice about family expectations. This dessert became a symbol of support and shared experience during a challenging time. Tres leches cake was more than just food; it was part of my community and coping mechanisms.

Turnip cake has always been one of my favorite Cantonese dishes, reminding me of the yum cha culture I grew up with in Hong Kong. Even though I moved away and my food experiences diversified, I still love turnip cake for its connection to my heritage. It’s a comforting reminder of where I come from.

Mango pudding is another Cantonese dessert I’ve always loved, tied to my memories of yum cha in Hong Kong. Despite living in Atlanta and trying many new foods, mango pudding remains a nostalgic favorite that connects me to my roots. It’s part of the culinary identity I cherish.

Learning to speak English fluently was a big part of my immigrant journey, especially after moving to Atlanta. I also learned to be proud of my mom’s broken English, which reflected her own struggles and resilience. English became a tool for me to navigate new environments and define my hyphenated identity.

Cheese grits were one of the Southern dishes I learned to make and enjoy after moving to Atlanta. They represent how I embraced Southern culture alongside my Cantonese heritage. Making cheese grits was part of how I blended different parts of my identity through food.

I replicated macaroni and cheese with ham from cha chaan teng, which connected me to both Southern and Hong Kong flavors. This dish showed how I merged different culinary traditions in my life. It was a way to honor my background while adapting to new tastes.

Cha chaan tengs are Hong Kong-style cafes where I used to enjoy dishes like macaroni with ham. They represent a unique part of Hong Kong’s food culture that I hold dear. Even after moving to Atlanta, I tried to recreate the flavors and memories of cha chaan teng at home.

Sweet tea is a classic Southern drink that I drank often after moving to Atlanta. It became part of my new food landscape and symbolized my growing appreciation for Southern culture. Sweet tea was one of the many ways I blended my heritage with my new environment.

Hong Kong-style milk tea is a staple of yum cha culture that I grew up loving. Drinking milk tea connects me to my Cantonese roots and the comforting rituals of my childhood. Even as my identity evolved, milk tea remained a delicious symbol of home.
